--- Extracted from upload: Final draft June From apprentice Boilermaker to Managing Director (002).pdf ---
Phil De Jager Rising fromRising fromApprentice toApprentice toManaging DirectorManaging Director  – My journey of growth and leadership 

© Phil de Jager 2025 South Africa.Publisher: www.sakurabookpublishing.comalta@sakurabookpublishing.comCover design: Alta H HaffnerISBN:                                    (print)                                             (e-book)All rights reserved. No part of this publicationmay be reproduced, distributed, or transmittedin any form or any means, includingphotocopying, recordings, or other electronic ormechanical methods without the prior writtenpermission of the author and publisher, except inthe case of brief quotations embodied in criticalreviews and certain noncommercial usespermitted by copyright law.

CONTENTSCONTENTSAbout Phil de JagerForewordEarly homelife and formative yearsStudy commencement Early career / sportCareer ContinuationOverseas ExposureManagement styleSporting careerIndustries I have worked inBusiness Learnings / Life ChallengesGood Business QuotesKey focus areas and life learnings Conclusion / SummaryTestimonials45791117273242485062647881

About Phil de JagerAbout Phil de JagerPhil De Jager is an accomplished business leader withover five decades of experience across the engineering,pharmaceutical, food, and manufacturing industries. Fromhis humble beginnings as an apprentice to becoming aManaging Director. Phil has led major businessturnarounds, international expansions, and strategictransformations. His passion for leadership, resilience,and people development is at the heart of his memoir,offering a compelling journey of growth and inspiration.4 

Foreword This book attempts to convince young and older people thatanything is possible if you believe in yourself. Don’t be swayed bywhere you come from, your poor childhood, your background, oranything that would deter progress or success. It is amazing whatcan be achieved via a positive mindset and focus. Decide what you would like to achieve early in life, so to be clear onpossible goals for your life. Based on the benefits of social mediaplatforms, research on poor or good careers and Companies towork for is easily accessible. So do some homework to make thebest possible decision early in life. I was just told what to do and didit with some regrets later, but that did not deter me from changingmy career. Remember it is your career, and you must take charge ofyour life while listening and acting on the best advice. The decisions you make could have long-lasting value. In my case,where I just completed a very successful contract at a big companyat the age of 73 and I can’t wait to start the next project. I had manychallenges, i.e. Friends distractions. Fortunately, I resisted peerpressure and focused on my studies and career. 5

I had a solid work ethic. A lesson I learned early in life was to beconsistent on a daily basis. Consistency leads to great success. I was always curious and willing to learn. I focused on improving my skills continuously. “The difference between the impossible and thepossible lies in a man's determination.” Tommy Lasorda Nothing is impossible, the worditself says I’M POSSIBLE.~ Aubrey Hepburn6

Early homelife andformative years My Dad was a miner, I was his youngest son and child of fivesiblings. It was the culture of the day that the sons should followin their dad's footsteps and also work on the mine which mybrother and I did. My older brother became an apprentice Boilermaker and after 5 years, passed his trade test whereafter hebecame a qualified Artisan. I was encouraged by his degree ofsuccess and stability and achievement. At the age of 16, I decidedto leave school, and like my brother, I became an apprenticeBoilermaker. It was possible at the age of 16 to do anapprenticeship. I had a poor upbringing as Miners did not earn much money and we stayed in semi-detached mine houses. We also moved from house to house in the same town. My mother and father were remarkably simple down-to-earth people with very little excitementin their lives. I respected them and loved them dearly. It was mostlywork and sleep every day. As the youngest by 7 years, my siblingsall left home and got married whilst I was a young boy living withmy Parents. My Mother was a very industrious stay-at-home parentand my dad spent most of his life underground as a miner. 7

My dad was a devoted Christian. We went to church every Sunday,which stood me in good stead until today, which my wife and Iconveyed to all our children. My dad was more outgoing, but mymother was a shy introvert. However, with an incredibly lowincome, it was amazing how they gave us a normal upbringing. Thissituation motivated me to be more successful and not to strugglelike my parents. They were extremely trustworthy people. As mentioned, I started working at the Sub Nigel gold Mine as an apprentice Boilermaker at the age of sixteen. (Still a young boy).However, this proved to be a good thing later in my life as I realisedquickly that I was not going to remain a Boilermaker and work likethis for the rest of my life. I soon decided that I was unique and did not have to follow in mydad’s footsteps of work in those terrible conditions, crawlingaround like a dog underground whilst inhaling dangerous dust,which killed him far too early. Fortunately, I excelled in all forms of sport as a young boy, which allowed me to become a top athlete later. The discipline required to be a top athlete also helped in my working career and studieslater. 8

 It is important to study to make progress in life, and it is beneficial for young people to recognize the value of dedicating some time to their education. Remember studying is a once-off event for a “fairly” brief time, but the benefits are lifelong. Witnessing the tough life of my parents motivated me to start my Engineering studies with the assistance of Mine management whilst serving my apprenticeship. I made particularly substantial progress and passed every term. I worked as hard as I could to show that I was not just a good Boilermaker, but also a goodstudent.Studycommencement “Although I enjoyed my job as a Boilermaker and the hand skills itgave me, I had bigger ambitions.”9You become what you study.-Robert T. Kiyosaki

I realised earlier that to be successful; I had to study as hard as Icould. Fortunately, I had a mother who pushed me, waking meearly in the morning, to succeed in my studies. The studies wentso well that by the age of nineteen, I reached the highest level ofmy engineering grades. I qualified after only 3 years to take mytrade test, which I did and passed with excellence, whichconfirmed that I was a top-class Boilermaker. Soon afterqualifying as a Boilermaker Artisan (age 19), I obtained myMechanical Engineering Diploma. I was the youngest artisan on the mine to obtain a diploma at theage of twenty as a qualified artisan. I was also the 1stBoilermaker to obtain a diploma in engineering. My dad wascalled from underground one morning to attend my diplomaceremony presented by the mine management. You can imaginehow proud he was of his son standing there, being a miner andbeing honoured. It was a day that I will never forget. I then knewthat I was unique and destined for bigger things than being aBoilermaker for the rest of my life. It is a lesson that youngpeople should learn. You must believe that you are unique andcan achieve remarkable things with the help of our HeavenlyFather. In later years, I completed a diploma in company direction and passed all final-year subjects of an International MBA. I also did an intensive MBA short course at the University of Cape Town – UCT.I need to mention that my future wife assisted me throughout mystudies and encouraged me tremendously.  10

Early career / Sport The engineer's diploma changed my life, and the mine managementtransferred me to another mine (Libanon Gold Mine) to become anEngineer in Training, which was incredibly good practical experienceand exposure. At the age of 22 (1973), I married my girlfriendAntonette, and we stayed on the mine property. Over the next 4years, we had two girls and a boy whilst I completed my Engineer-in-Training period. This training assisted me in applying for andobtaining a good engineering job at the chemical giant AECI inSasolburg, which was successful. Another reason for me leavingmining was the fact that my dad died from a lung disease after allthe time underground in the mine. The time at the chemical company was an incredible experience fora young engineer as the plant operated 24 / 7 every week, itbrought severe pressure to my personal life due to the time spent atwork. However, it was a wonderful experience and great exposure. Iwas one of fifteen engineers at AECI. I was appointed manager ofthe VCM plant. After 3 years, I was given the job of supervising the repair of one of our leaking reactors in Germany. This was a wonderful experience 11

as I was fortunate to attend the initial meetings with seniorengineers of AECI to agree on the required specifications for thisreactor repair. We then went to Germany to agree on theprocedure with the Germans in Dusseldorf and Munich. I stayedbehind in Germany for the next few months to execute this repair ata selected German Company. The pressure was severe as AECI was losing big money every daywith the reactor out of service. With this knowledge, I convincedthe Germans to work night shift on this job. The job was done 30days early, saving R30 million, back in the eighties. I contacted AECISouth Africa with this good news, which I kept a secret until it wascompleted. You can imagine the welcome I had when I returned toSouth Africa earlier than expected. The company surprised me witha big welcoming party. After that successful outcome, additional responsibilities came myway, and I was appointed as technical manager of the cyanide andperoxide plants of AECI and the VCM plant. I managed to sort outthe serious issues that caused lots of downtime on the cyanideplant, as it was a strategic site due to supplying cyanide to the goldmines in the process of gold extraction. My corrective actionincreased output by 50%, which allowed an adequate supply ofcyanide to the gold mines. The experience at AECI was priceless.After completing several cost-reduction projects, I recognised mystrong analytical skills. 12

One Sunday, I happened to scroll through the job section of ourlocal Sunday newspaper and came across a job vacancy for aCompany Engineer in the largest pharmaceutical company inSouth Africa – Adcock Ingram. I was incredibly happy at AECI butdecided (as a joke) to apply for this Job. It was a major shock tome when I got the call for an interview at Adcock Ingram. Idecided to go for the interview more out of curiosity, as you canimagine, I could not leave AECI, especially after the recentGerman project and promotion. However, I went for the interview and was surprised at how well itwent, especially due to my AECI experience, which counted in my favor. I did not realise that AECI was recognised as such an important benchmark in business. I was driving in my car, back to my office just after the interview, when I received a call from Adcock Ingram and they offered me the Job. I got such a fright that I declined the Job and returned to AECI. Back in my office, I received another call, this time with an offer that I knew I could not decline. I then communicated to the AECI executive that I was resigning, which was a big shock to them in light of my recent successful trip to Germany. They tried everything possible to retain my services, but I made up my mind. I was then working for AECI for a 13

period of 5 Years. Also, at Adcock Ingram Pharmaceuticals, I wouldbe the only Company Engineer. During this period, my mother died a year after my father at an early age of 54, compared to my father, who died at 63 years. She could not live without him. During my time pursuing new careers, my lovely wife had the tough task of raising our 3 kids. Another boy was born 7 years after ouryoungest child. We were blessed, we had a Family of 2 boys and 2girls in our twenties and enjoying a successful career. In the end, I had a very successful career of 14 years at Adcock Ingram. I was extremely fortunate to have been allowed to rebuild/design a new layout for the packing plant. The company then sent me to Germany to be trained on the latest sterile equipment that we purchased for our sterile division. I came back and trained all the technical staff. I went to Boston in the USA where I was trained on a reverseosmosis plant that we purchased to improve the water quality inour sterile division. After a phenomenally successful period at thepharmaceutical plant, I was promoted to Technical Director andgiven responsibility for the Consumer division as well. The totalsupply chain was added to my new role, i.e., Distribution,Production, Procurement, etc.  14

My MD called me in and sent me to Scotland to investigate thepossibility of installing the Jeyes Blue line in South Africa, as wewere importing the product. I installed this new Plant in SouthAfrica the following year, which enabled us to produce the productat a much lower price locally. The continuous development and innovation were enormous at Adcock Ingram, as I witnessed continuous new product launches. The manufacturing discipline that was required in a pharmaceutical factory required good manufacturing disciplines as we were producing products that could kill people, hence proper validations and good housekeeping were paramount. I learned to apply the Japanese culture of “full proof”, No mistakes called “Poka-Yoke”, as mistakes are not allowed in the pharmaceutical process. This focus will assist you in your future career, as mistakes in business can be costly. Between career challenges, I developed into a top tennis player, winning numerous tournaments. I also became a 3 handicap golfer, playing tournaments all over South Africa. At a later age, I also became the Alberton Tennis and Squash Champion in one year, which was an outstanding achievement at the age of 36. Focusing on 3 types of sports was not easy, and today I regret thehours I spent away from my wife and children playing sports.  15

I must apologise to my wife and children today for being absent. Iwould advise young people to focus on fewer sports to restore andmaintain a good family life balance while being married. During myfirst trip to Germany, I started coaching the factory staff for 2months as I was the Vaal Triangle Tennis Champion in South Africa. There were hours of hard work and discipline to achieve these sporting results. However, the discipline required to train hard was also evident in my success at work. I was also fortunate to work for incredibly good bosses who were also my mentors and assisted in my early development. 16Motivation gets you going, butdiscipline keeps you growing.-John C. MaxwellDiscipline is doing what needsto be done, even if you don’twant to do it.

Career Continuation In the meantime, my Career / Business life progressed extremelywell. I was always very goal-driven to achieve the best, whichstood me in good stead as superior results meant good salaryincreases. I think that the competitiveness in sports assisted metremendously to also be competitive in my job and always strivefor the best, i.e., meeting agreed KPI’s. I hope young people take this lesson to heart that you must believe in your uniqueness and know that, with faith in our Heavenly Father, you can achieve great things. These experiences over 14 years set me up for future career developments, as South African executives were aware of my success at Adcock Ingram and AECI. The boss at Adcock Ingram was a great mentor to me, not just teaching me technical skills, but also trusting me in giving me more and more opportunities locally and abroad. I was, however, surprised to be contacted and headhunted by the Chairman of a big export group who offered me the position of Managing Director in Port Elizabeth, South Africa, after 14 years 17

with Adcock Ingram and no plans to change companies. He made itclear to me that, based on my success at Adcock Ingram, he wasmaking me an offer. I was then 42 Years old. Again, I had my doubtsabout leaving Adcock Ingram as I was extremely happy in thecompany, but the offer was of such a nature that I had to accept it.This took me to the ultimate position of Managing Director in alarge group, which is logical based on my career achievements atthat stage. I felt immensely proud as I realised that I had progressed from theyoung apprentice boilermaker to Managing Director.Unfortunately, we had to leave 2 Children behind in JHB to move toPort Elizabeth, a decision that we regretted later. The move to PortElizabeth was also traumatic, hence away from the Family inGauteng. The new job was incredibly challenging as it involved total exports to Italy, France, Japan, Malaysia, and Europe. The Managing Director in this business makes all the key decisions on quality, procurement and selling prices. He also oversaw key negotiations with key suppliers in South Africa and overseas. Everything I knowabout negotiations I learned at this company is that I had tonegotiate all over the world. I was away from home frequently,but fortunately, my wife was able to join me on occasional trips. 18

The 5 years with the group were good learning as I had the totalmanagement team reporting to me, hence good financial and globalmarket experience. Purchasing was especially important as we hadto buy all our raw materials from the local farmers, which were the”Dorper” sheep skins. The leather fairs were held in Paris, France,twice a year. Once finally tanned in Europe, these skins were sold astop-quality Leather garments across the world. The company wasunfortunately sold to the larger opposition after 5 years, and we gotoffered good severance packages, which I accepted. This meant that we had to return to Gauteng to a happy Family. The biggest Food Company in South Africa, Tiger Brands, recruited me to manage their edible oil business, SA Oil Mills in Randfontein in Gauteng. The company was owned 50/50 by Tiger Brands and Senwes, a Farmers co-op. Tiger Brands wanted to exit commodity businesses, hence joined forces with Senwes, to transform the business back to profitability to maximise the selling price. The key objective was my responsibility, and I had to prepare a “blue Paper” for this business transformation. Two weeks later, I submitted my findings/ proposals, which the company, Senwes, accepted immediately and became the holding Company as they purchased Tiger Brands 50%. The proposal included a Capex of 10 million rand to enable automation of the packing operation, which was very labor-intensive and was approved. In summary, Tiger Brands and 19

Senwes’ edible oil plants were operating separately, hence totalduplication of operations, i.e., a crushing (Crude oil) plant atSenwes and Tiger Brands, Refineries at Senwes and Tiger andseparate oil packing operations. This situation led me to make asimple proposal to rationalise all operations, i.e. One Crude oilplant at Senwes and one refinery at Tiger Brands, with one newautomated packing operation at Tiger Brands. This meant seriousstaff reduction, and we had to follow an ethical retrenchmentprocess. I had the privilege to manage this total process, which achieved all the goals in my blueprint. I have met the cost reduction target, and the Indian consortium Continental Oils bought the company from Senwes.. I chose not to work for the new Company due to a different culture. However, Senwes afforded me a good package for my efforts. This was the start of my Business Transformation focus up until today. My overseas exposure blessed me with the ability to see the big picture. Today, I can walk into any business and my analysis will indicate major improvements and cost savings. Based on the great export exposure with my previous company I was recruited back to Adcock Ingram to become Director of Exports into Africa, Europe and the Far East. I was part of a small team with export sales not significant. My knowledge of the Adcock Ingram 20

products made this a logical move. I then persuaded the TigerBrands management to put all products into one basketinclusive of Adcock Ingram who at that stage was part of TigerBrands. My total export basket then consisted ofpharmaceuticals, food products, and Beacon Sweetsconfectionary products. Three years later we grew into a muchlarger export company based on focus on one export company.Again, I enjoyed the exposure and I was also selling TigerBrands products, the biggest food company in South Africa.Confectionary was a big part of them. It gave me a massiverange of products to sell over the next few years with manyoverseas trips. I travelled extensively to Africa, Europe and the Far East. I alsosold products in Cyprus Greece very successfully.Unfortunately, the local market grew at such a pace that thelocal factories were unable to supply our export salesconsistently, which throttled the export business. It wasobvious that local companies received preference. The marketsthat I established died as we could not supply products to ourcustomers until the RSA factories increased their capacitieswhich would take too long. I decided to leave the export business for this reason and started using my expertise in transforming companies and I Joined a company called Retailer Brands to do this. After transforming this company, I was asked to join a confectionary group in East London 21

South Africa called Candy Tops to transform them. I joined them asTechnical Director. This was one of the very few negative moves inmy career, as I realized that the candy product margins wereextremely small, and we had to be very smart to make a profit.However, this gave me great confectionary exposure and theexposure to work for a loss-making company for the 1st time whichI would need in my future career. Candy Tops merged with their sister Company Mr. Sweet; hence it closed the East London operations and some products were transferred to Mr. Sweet in JHB. I was offered a package which I accepted. However, my confectionary experience stood me in good stead as I was contacted again by Tiger Brands in South Africa. Their confectionary businesses had serious problems after a failed project installation with a cost of R 200 million, and they recruited me on a contract basis to rectify the problems and restore the factory performance. They could not get the main gums & jellies line called a mogul to operate at the desired efficiency. I installed a real-time production monitoring system to measure the performance  24/7. It took me 6 months to restore this line successfully. I was recruited for a period of 6 months but stayed for a period of 4 Years and was promoted to head up the supply chainat the confectionery business after all the success. This was an 22

intensive study in which I applied Pareto technology to identify the20% that caused 80% of the problems. I not only rectified theirproblems but also improved productivity in all the confectionaryplants in the gums & jellies division. I am very proud to say that itwas a very successful contract period with great results and myrelationship with this group continued at a later stage. At that stage the Tiger Brands executive who recruited me to Tiger Brands became the Supply Chain Director of a beverage company called BEVCO in Johannesburg and at the end of my contract heasked me to come and assist him with this beverage company.Again, I immediately got involved in transforming the company andmade improvements as the beverage business is also verycompetitive, hence cost savings via improved productivity improvements were critical. I spent a few years under contract with this company and achieved all my goals. I was then contacted by Tiger Brands to become the Supply Chain Director for a contract period. I was given the responsibility of all confectionary plants including gums & jellies, candy and  chocolate plants. This job had big responsibilities with a workforce of 1400 wages and 40 management staff reporting to me. I did not have to worry too much about the gums & jellies plant as I transformed it during my time there as I described earlier. 23

My focus shifted to the candy and chocolate operations withchocolate being the important one from a profitability point of view. I had an unbelievable 14 Months, transforming these plants successfully and I was awarded a full bonus at the end of mystay. Productivity was improved, food safety standards were met and improved, and we retained all required quality certifications. Housekeeping was upgraded and it was like walking through a good hospital. The people were collaborating extremely well as they enjoyed my management style of “Trust and Inspire” not “Command and Control.” A highlight in my career was the staff jumping up and down and celebrating my return. It was incredible. I also installed a Real-time production monitoring system “to measure OEE’s - Overall Equipment Efficiency. At the end of this contract, Tiger Brands asked me to transform their Black Cat peanut butter plant. This was a very interesting assignment as I managed to not only transform the business like the other ones, but they had a product processing problem linked to the quality of raw materialsthat I sorted out and the output from this plant increasedtremendously. All standards as implemented at Tiger Brands plantswere implemented in the Peanut Butter plant. Another verysuccessful contract period and full bonus paid. 24

Just note all this work was done as a contractor as I have now beenworking 8 years after my retirement age (65) and I am now 73 yearsold. I learned the importance of “word of mouth” contacts Thelesson is that you are never too old to be successful if you producequality work. I was then headhunted by a Company in the Powder cereal business called Future Life. This is a company that has been growing by 10 to 20 % per annum in the last ten years. They had to move from the old premises that became too small to a new site in Durban. They then required new expertise to assist with the “new” business. Their old site did not comply with food safety standards and many standards were lacking. I relished the new 17 Months contract which allowed me to exercise my full experience within this business. I just had the most successful contract period with the business and the results improved tremendously with massive bottom-line savings and profitability improvement. I also installed a real-time production monitoring system which simplifies the operation tremendously. Production capacity was identified as a major problem as the plantworked 6 days a week. I installed a new high-speed packing linewhich now allowed us to only work 4 days a week, hence majorlabor savings. Our food safety standards were upgraded and productivity improved. I just completed my contract at the end of  March.25

Who knows what is next! I am establishing my own BusinessTransformation Company via LinkedIn to reach many influentialleaders who can contact me for business transformation projects.I also have superb testimonies that will stand me in good stead. It should be mentioned that I was responsible for industrial relations with all these companies, making great experience in theannual wage negotiations. Another important issue is the one of relationship building as in your future career, you might have to lean on some of these relationships forged early in your career. I also made sure that I finished off on a high note at every companyI worked for as you could one day need them again. You also leave agood legacy with each company. 26

Overseas exposure I was very fortunate to travel abroad many times for businessrelated issues. During my early career with AECI, I was sent to Germany to fix one of the main reactors for a few months. It was great to experience new technology in Germany, hence expanding my engineering mindset and knowledge. Adcock Ingram then sent me to Boston in the USA to select and purchase a new Reverse Osmosis plant for our sterile division where we needed RO water. They also sent me to Germany to purchase new sterile machinery to upgrade our technology. At a later stage, I then trained all the technical staff before the new machines arrived. Adcock Ingram sent me to The Jeyes operation in East Kilbride Scotland to copy their Plant in South Africa which I completed very successfully and produced a better plant than in Scotland. As part of a business transformation at a company called Continental Oils I went to Italy to commission equipment that I bought for the company to reduce costs via a major laborproductivity upgrade.27

The project was so successful that the ompany changed from a lossto a profit situation. My time as Managing Director of Kolosus Holdings took me on many overseas trips i.e. Korea, China, Malaysia, Italy, France etc. Back at Adcock Ingram, I was appointed as Export Director for Africa and Asia. I conducted several trips to Africa and Asia and our export Sales improved by 200% year on year. I started a new business in Cyprus in Greece where I sold key Tiger Brands products. I was also invited by a Korean Pharmaceutical company to assess their new Pharmaceutical Plant in Korea. All these overseas trips were incredible as I could apply manyof the learnings in our local market to the benefit of the Companies that I upgraded. Today I am extremely grateful to my lovely wife of 51 years who had to look after the children during all my overseas trips and sporting activities. She had a lot to do with improving my discipline, persistence and dedication to being successful. Her constant prayers and unselfishness are something I can never appreciate enough. She had a lot to do with the disciplined upbringing ofour children and constant relationship with God which is themost important thing to all of us. He made all things possible. Ican strongly advise young management to get overseasexposure as it broadens your benchmark standards.  28

29Phil & Antonette de JagerPhil & Antonette de Jager

 Countries and Cities visitedfor Business and Holidays Germany – Munich, Dusseldorf and Krefeld USA – Boston France – Paris. Italy – Rome, Capri, Venice, Solofra, Napoli Holland - Amsterdam, Vollendam Scotland – East Killbride, England – Londen, Manchester Greece – Cyprus Zimbabwe - Harare Angola – Luanda Ghana – Acra China – Shanghai, Beijing Korea – Seoul Japan – Tokyo, Kobe Singapore. Taiwan Malaysia – Kuala Lumpur Thailand – Bangkok Mauritius Namibia / Botswana MozambiqueZanzibar 30

31A leader is best when peoplebarely know he exists. Whenhis work is done, his aimfulfilled, they will say: wedid it ourselves.-Lao Tzu

Management style  “A leader is one who knows the way, goes the way, and shows the way." - John C. Maxwell I always believed that it was critical to have the correct/effective Management style. I do not believe in a Command-and-Control style which results in staff fearing their leaders and must constantly look over their shoulders for being micromanaged. I promote a style of Trust and Inspire which allows people to Innovate and use their full potential to the benefit of their companies without the fear of failure. Too many businesses suffer from poor results due to poor management styles. The CEO of Microsoft took over at an embattled Microsoft in 2014 and turned the business around from a market value of 300 BN USD to 2 trillion USD today. Poor management must be identified early in a company and be eliminated. During my career, I have found most managers were trained in the technical side of their Jobs, but not in good management practices or they exit university without required management training. The ability to uplift their subordinates is lacking and toachieve results via the people. 32

 “Ghandi was saying that human beings only use a small percentage of what they are capable of and the secret lies in extracting people’s capability via a Trust and Inspire management style.” Their staff is not being trained, hence not trusted to do a good job.It was very clear to me in my career, that when there were staffproblems to find out who was managing the people and withoutexception poor management would be the reason.33People don’t leave bad jobs... They leavebecause of bad bosses, and poor management,who don’t appreciate their value.-Marcus BuckinghamToday is your opportunity to build thetomorrow you want.-Ken PoirotThe bottom line is, bad managers are badfor business, and they’re even worse fortheir employees.-Arnold Smeets

Poor management is in most cases the key reason for businessfailure or poor performance. Any problem can be traced back tothe quality of management and the lack of a good leader. Toomany managers are just managing instead of leading people. “The challenge of leadership is to be strong, but not rude; be kind, but not weak; be bold, but not a bully; be humble, but not timid; be proud, but not arrogant; have humor but without folly.” “The art of leadership is saying no, not saying yes. It is very easy to say  yes.” “The workforce demands a new kind of leader – a leader who can see develop and unleash the greatness in every person “ When I left the company Tiger Brands, one of the senior managers came to me and thanked me for giving them wings to fly, based on my delegating style. That confirmed my success with utilizing people effectively while they develop. Another important thing to have is “Passion”. I am 73 but have lost none of my passion. Where there is passion there is a belief thatall things are possible. 34

People with the right attitude are critical, in some cases, it is betterto appoint a person with the right attitude instead of betterqualifications. The other embarrassing observation during my career was the fact that the floor staff in a factory remains employed for many years i.e. 20 years, but management changes every 2 to 3 years. Every new manager or Executive arrives with “new Ideas” that disappear over a year or two and they are gone. Shopfloor staff questioned me many times about this, and it has become a laughing matter. Only a few Executives can motivate and uplift their staff and walk a long sustainable journey with them. Many managers are also not in touch with the needs of their people, hence managing or leading people they don’t know. They also don’t invite ideas and inputs from the lower-level staff who do all the work at floor level whilst management sits in their Ivory Towers. Emotional intelligence is critical when managing or leading people. The importance of handling relationships correctly is very important. 35

 ” Being a leader is a privilege to have. Your job is about beingable to help people to achieve their best potential.” “People have greatness inside of them – so my job as a leader is to unleash their potential – not control the me” “It is a privilege, not an entitlement, to lead” “Vulnerability is not a weakness; it is our greatest measure of courage” “People will forget what you have said, people will forget what you have done, but they will never forget how you made them feel” 36Emotional intelligence is your ability torecognise and understand emotions inyourself and others, and your ability to usethis awareness to manage your behaviourand relationships.-Travis Bradberry

It is also good to identify mentors throughout your career. Thesemust be trusted by high-integrity colleagues who can give youhonest feedback on a regular basis, and vice versa. This network willstand you in good stead later in your career. In my experience, many companies don’t have clear strategies or goals for their business or departments. For example, no strategy toimprove their Supply Chain. Companies complete their annualbudgets without any strategic preparation like a “Bos Beraad“ or“think tank” to agree on the next year’s strategy. There might bestructure changes based on the new strategy, but not foreseen inthe budget.Mission and Vision statements are also absent in many businesses. The one that I find intriguing is the comment or advertisement in Companies that “People are our most important assets” However when a business gets in financial difficulty people the “most important “ assets are being dismissed first.? Nobody in the management is investigating how to grow the business instead of closing it down. Remember structure follows strategy.  Many companies do notchange their structure after changing their strategy, thenimplementation becomes a problem37

There will also be times in your career when you will have to takerisks i.e. making critical decision/s. You will gain experience fromthe outcome right or wrong. “We take risks knowing that risks will sometimes result in failure, but without the possibility of failure, there is no possibility of success. “ Remember the book called “Failing Forward” The most successful people in the world failed many times, but they learned from it. It is not a shame to fail. It is also important to embrace new technologies as the world is changing so rapidly today, i.e. AI technology. Finally, the importance of good communication is critical. It is so important that the company strategy and goals are clear to the total workforce. 38

39Effective communication in theworkplace isn’t just about speakingclearly, it’s about ensuring yourmessage is heard and understood.-Sheryl SandbergIn every thriving workplace,communication is the thread that weavessuccess.-Anna Richards

 As you take on more responsibility in your career, you will have bigger teams reporting to you, hence the importance of having the right people under you. People that you can delegate to, and trust all your goals will be achieved. This is where the Trust and Inspirestyle is so important. All levels of staff should be empowered all thetime. It is also very important to celebrate success down to the shop floor. In many companies, the Management team might celebrate, but the shopfloor never benefits i.e. the people who do a lot of work. I had a banner at the entrance of my company in Port Elizabethwhere I was the Managing Director which read “WORK SMART ANDHAVE FUN” and we applied it.  40The art of communication is thelanguage of leadership.-James Humes

Sporting career  Sports played a major part in my life and even at 73 today I love watching sports and going to the Gym on a regular basis. Rememberthat none of this will be possible without good health, hencekeeping a steady diet and regular training is critical. I showed greattalent at school as I played Rugby, did well in athletics participatedat a high level of Gymnastics where I earned a silver medal at atraining camp. As a result, I became a very good trampolinist. However, at a later stage in school, I got interested in Tennis via my sister and brother-in-law who played tennis. I did not realize that this would become my main sport in which I excelled and won about 20 Tiles throughout my career. I also had success at theprovincial level and won the prestigious Vaal Triangle championshipfor 4 years. During my AECI business trip to Germany, I had to coachthe total company staff for 2 Months in Dusseldorf.I had a tennis court at home and coached about 60 pupils every week. At one stage I coached all the Champions in their age groups i.e. under 12, 14, 16 and 18 years old. At that stage, I was playing 1st League tennis. My two best wins were beating the Free state no.1 player doing a provincial match and beating the 41

South African No.1 under 16 player called Pieter Aldrich who laterwon international tournaments and were in the top ten Men’sdouble players in the world. During this time, I also started playing Golf and became a 3-handicap player within 2 years. My best tournament score was71. Gross score at the Macau vlei Golf course in Vereeniging where Iwon the competition. I was also fortunate to play on the worldrenowned Glen Eagles course in Scotland. The 2014 Ryder Cup was played on that course. In my late 20's due to being a good tennis player I started playing squash and excelled rapidly as I enjoyed the speed of the game and the associated fitness. At that stage, I was the Alberton tennis champion for a few years. One night after a game of squash the top players were discussing who was going to be the squash champion later that year. Very bravely I threw my hat in the mix with the prediction that I would win both the Tennis and Squash titles of Alberton Clubs in that year. This prediction was a bit presumptuous as I was the no. 44 player on the ladder at the squash club. I am using this story to show you young people that if you put your mind to something (even the impossible) it can be achieved. 42

Without going into too much detail I started doing roadworki.e. jogging and sprinting for the next few months before theChampionships in September of 87. The Tennis championshipwas 1st and I won it comfortably as I was extremely fit trainingmainly for the Squash championship. During that year I improved my position on the squash ladder from 44 to the top ten. A few weeks later I was crowned the new Alberton squash Champion after the Tennis title and my wish came true. A lesson to many out there that you can achieve anything you put tour mind to. Some of my Saturdays consisted of squash from 6 to 7 am Saturday mornings, thereafter 18 holes of Golf from 8 am to 1 pm and then 1st league Tennis that afternoon. However, this sports discipline as indicated stood me in good stead in my business career where I could apply the same discipline, dedication and competitiveness. I also think this has a lot to do with the fact that I am still managing big companies as a Transformation Specialist at the age of 73 and still achieving great results. 43

44What you do today can improve all yourtomorrows.-Ralph MarstonSelf-discipline starts with the mastery ofyour thoughts. If you don’t control what youthink, you can’t control what you do.-Napoleon Hill

 Unique Life moments  The day I got married to my lovely wife Antonette The Days when each of children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren were born. Presented Mr. Mandela with a cheque for his 75th birthdaycelebrations in Port Elizabeth.Spent an evening with ex-Protea Captain Hansie Cronje as he wasour speaker for the evening Spent a day on the Great Wall of China.45Discipline is the bridge betweengoals and accomplishments.-Jim Rohn

I was taken through the Murray Field Scotland and their Rugbyclubhouse and stood in the center of the field. Played 18 holes of Golf on the famous Ryder Cup GleneaglesGolf course in Scotland. Being taken to a Tennis tournament in a Pontiac Firebird by anAmerican Family. Scoring a hole-in-one at the Vereeniging Country Club andwinning a Golf competition with a score of 71 gross from an 8handicap at Macau Vlei Golf Club. The day when my dad joined me in congratulations on passing myTrade test and was awarded an Engineering diploma by the Mineexecutive.Passing the MBA final year exam. Trips to Germany, Scotland, England, Italy, France, Holland, China,Japan, Korea, Boston USA, Malaysia, Taiwan, Singapore, HongKong, Bali, Phuket Thailand, Kenya, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Ghana,Botswana, Mozambique, Zanzibar, Cyprus. Many with my lovelywife. A Weekend visit to the Isle of Capri in Italy. Attended a Church service with my son Philip at Yonggi Chochurch in South Korea. Spending time with my two sons in China / Korea.  Attended the 1995 Rugby World Cup win at Ellispark with myManagement team as MD 46

Seeing my daughters and sons winning Tennis tournaments. The day I returned to Tiger Brands in Durban and saw the staffcelebrating my return. The day when Lizel was saved during a serious car accident andPhilip was hit by a car and Charlene survived a bomb scare whilstin a day clinic and also saved me from a serious incident at AECI. The day my youngest son Anton was chosen to play squash forSouth Africa and became a prefect at the High School. The day Anton’s life was saved after a horrific car accident. The quality of my mother and my Wife’s food-making ability. The day when I gave my heart to my lord Jesus, and he savedme. The weekly communications with my brother for more than 50Years. I ran 5 km in 19 minutes and 30 seconds training for the squashchampionship. 47

Industries I have worked in My career path was extremely interesting as described. See below the list of different businesses from start to current : Gold Mines as Engineer in Training – JHB – 5 Years Chemical Plant – AECI as Engineer on projects – JHB – Engineer     – 5 Years Pharmaceuticals/Consumer Products– Adcock Ingram laboratories– JHB – Technical Director – 14 Years  Sunflower oil – SA Oil Mills – JHB – OPS Director – 4 Years Leather Production and total exports – Kolosus – Port Elizabeth    – Managing Director – 4 Years Consumer Products exports – Adcock Ingram / Tiger Brands –Director Exports – 2 Years Consumable products ( Soups ) - Retailer Brands – JHB - OPSDirector – 1 Year Confectionary products – Candy Tops – East London – OPSDirector.- 4 Years Confectionary Products – Tiger Brands – Durban – Ops Director –2 Years Beverage – BEVCO - JHB – Ops Director – 1 Year 48

Confectionary ( All Plants) – Tiger Brands – Durban – Ops Director.- 1 year Tiger Brands Black Cat peanut butter plant – Randfontein– JHB - Ops Director - 1 year Supply Chain head -Future life – Durban – Powder porridges– (Current) – 17 Months You will notice that towards the end of my career, my durationat companies were short due to the contract periods appointedto do business transformation. 49

Business Learnings / Life Challenges: Do a proper investigation into the Company you would like towork for i.e. Workplace safety, People culture, location, Producttypes, Financial stability, Management style, Incentives, etc. Working for a loss-making company can be very negative as thegeneral thinking is one of cost-cutting and not growth. The Company must add value to your career.When you leave a company remember the grass is not alwaysgreener on the other side, hence you must leave for good reasonsand do your homework.Determine whether the Management style is “Command &Control” i.e. micromanagement or they have a “Thrust & Inspire”style. This can influence your progress in a Company as someManagement styles will not allow you to innovate or develop.Determine whether companies do have development programsfor you so that you grow in every Company where you work.Analyze your Company fully so that you understand the totalbusiness. In this way, you can add value to the business.50

 Identify companies that might allow you overseas exposure andexperience. This exposure will stand you in good stead as your global mindsetdevelops. Join companies that are stable, and where you can see your futuredevelopment. Ensure that you grow your business knowledge/experience in everyCompany you work for. Surround yourselves with the “right” people to assist you. If youtrust and inspire these people, your results will be excellent.51Ability is what you’re capable of doing.Motivation determines what you do.Attitude determines how well you do it.-Lou Holtz

 Don’t “carry” people that don’t want to improve, (even aftertraining ) . The wrong person will take up a great percentage of yourmanagement time. Incentivize staff, if possible, to achieve urgent goals. The cost of doing things must always be self-funding.Decide whether you want to work for a big corporate company(maybe more security, but more controls and “red” tape) or for asmaller company where your inputs might be valued more, butpossibly less security.Hire effective staff that can add value from day one, but alsodevelop new staff to add value at a later stage. Learn to celebrate success, so that staff can see that the place theywork for is not just working smart but are also having fun.Becoming a Managing Director is like entering a new world i.e.suddenly you have many more managers from various disciplinesreporting to you for direction. This is where a Trust and Inspirestyle added tremendous value. I chose to trust the Managers under me as being the specialists intheir jobs and I focused on the Trust and Inspired part of it. 52

Maintaining your work-life balance is very important. Too many managers are taking work home. The reason for this is the possible lack of good planning or timemanagement. Procrastination is a big sin with many people, they take too longto get things done. In the case of really too much work, work should be delegated toothers to assist, but it is critical to spend quality time with yourfamily.Management fails to realize that for a small financial cost, a lotof good work can be done.It's a relief to know that Anton recovered after such a difficulttime and has gone on to build a successful career as a SeniorIT programmer. 53

 There was another challenging time when the companies Iworked for closed. The staff uncertainty was enormous and good and honestcommunication during this time was critical. The anxiety to get the next job was always worrying, but Godsupplied in every instance. I learned early in my career on project work to under promise andover delivery. If you say the project will take 6 Months and you do it in 7 monthsmanagement frowns, however, if you said 8 Months, but took 7Months (Same result) you are a hero. The saying that failing to plan leads to a failed plan is so true. The Western world doesn’t plan long enough, and is slow withimplementing, whereas the Japanese culture spends a long timeplanning but implementing quickly. 54

55Recognising people’s good work isn’t just about praise—it’s aboutreinforcing the behaviours and values that drive organisationalexcellence. Celebrating results is how leaders show that effort, perseverance, andcollaboration matter. When people feel seen, appreciated, andconnected to a larger purpose, they become more engaged, more loyal,and more inspired to contribute at their best.This principle is grounded in three key leadership beliefs:Recognition fuels motivation. A simple "well done" can be more powerful than a bonus. It buildsconfidence and reinforces a sense of value and belonging.Celebration creates culture. Taking time to honour milestones—big or small—strengthens teamcohesion and embeds a culture of achievement.Visible leadership sets the tone. When leaders actively notice and acknowledge excellence, it signalsto everyone that high standards and accountability are non-negotiable.Celebrate Excellence: “Recognising Contributions and Amplifying Success"

56Recognising the Invisible Win – A Lesson from the Factory FloorIt was during my tenure at Adcock Ingram, right in the middle of a majorsupply chain overhaul, when I learned one of my most valuableleadership lessons—not from a boardroom, but from a boiler room.We had been struggling with downtime in one of our critical productionlines. A persistent maintenance issue had become a thorn in the side ofour operations team, affecting output and morale. While the leadershipteam was consumed with strategic planning and broader turnaroundgoals, it was Sipho, a soft-spoken artisan fitter, who quietly took theinitiative to diagnose the root cause.He worked after hours, pouring over manuals and consulting retiredcolleagues. Within days, he proposed a small but elegant solution—aminor redesign to the sealing system that eliminated the fault. The linereturned to full productivity. No fanfare. No formal recognition. Justresults.When I found out, I asked him to join the next management meeting—not to report, but to be recognised. I shared his story with the broaderteam, highlighting how his ownership, curiosity, and initiative embodiedthe culture we aspired to build. We gave him a framed certificate, butmore importantly, we gave him a voice. He walked out of that meeting adifferent man—and I believe, a future leader was born that day.That moment taught me this: Recognition isn’t about ceremony—it’sabout dignity. And celebration isn’t a distraction from work. It’s whatgives it meaning.

57Not to be affected by peerpressure from friends orother people meansDeveloping the confidence and self-awareness to make decisions basedon your own values, goals, and judgment—rather than simply followingwhat others expect or demand of you. It involves staying true to whoyou are, even when those around you may be doing something differentor trying to influence your choices.This doesn’t mean ignoring good advice or being stubborn. Rather, it’sabout critically evaluating suggestions and not compromising yourintegrity or direction just to fit in or gain approval. Peer pressure cancome in subtle forms, such as the fear of missing out (FOMO), the desireto be liked, or the discomfort of going against the group. Resisting itrequires inner strength, clarity about your own principles, andsometimes, the courage to stand alone.By not being swayed by peer pressure, you maintain control over yourown life. You set your own standards, choose your own path, and earnrespect for being authentic and principled..

58Leadership Insight: The Strength to Stand ApartOne of the most critical lessons I’ve learned on my journey is theimportance of not being swayed by peer pressure—whether fromcolleagues, friends, or even the broader industry. Leadership oftenrequires standing firm in your convictions, especially when the easierpath would be to go along with the crowd.There were times in my career when popular opinion leaned in onedirection, but my experience, instincts, and values pointed anotherway. In those moments, I had to decide whether to blend in or speakout. Choosing the latter was not always easy, but it was always right.True leadership isn't about popularity—it's about responsibility,integrity, and the courage to act in alignment with your principles.Peer pressure doesn’t end in schoolyards; it exists in boardrooms,team meetings, and social circles. But when you build a strong senseof self and a clear vision of what you stand for, you're better equippedto make decisions that reflect your true purpose—not someone else'sexpectations.

59Use Your Time Effectively – We Don’t Have Time to WasteTime is the one resource we all receive in equal measure—24 hours in aday. Yet, how we choose to use it determines the quality and impact ofour lives. In leadership, in business, and in personal growth, time isoften the hidden variable behind success and failure.We often say, “There’s not enough time,” when in truth, time isconstant; it’s our focus, priorities, and discipline that vary. The realchallenge lies not in finding more hours, but in eliminating what doesn'tserve our purpose.The Cost of Wasted TimeEvery hour wasted is an opportunity lost—an opportunity to learn, tolead, to build, to connect, or to rest and rejuvenate purposefully.Wasted time adds up. A few minutes of indecision here, anunproductive meeting there, and soon days, weeks, or even years driftby with little to show for it.In leadership roles, this inefficiency is amplified. Your misuse of time notonly affects your outcomes, but those of your team, your business, andsometimes even your industry.Time Discipline as a Competitive AdvantageHigh performers share a common trait: they respect their time fiercely.They are intentional with their calendars. They delegate well, automatewhat can be, and prioritize high-impact activities. They understand thatsaying “yes” to the wrong things is saying “no” to what truly matters.As a managing director or turnaround specialist, one of your first leversis time. Time audits, tighter agendas, focused deliverables—thesechanges may seem small, but they create momentum. You reclaimcontrol not just of your schedule, but of your business trajectory.

60Reflection from ExperienceThroughout my journey from apprentice to managing director, I learnedthis lesson repeatedly. The moments I grew the most—whetherlaunching a new product line, leading a turnaround, or mentoring afuture leader—were rarely the result of chance. They came fromfocused effort, ruthless prioritization, and the decision to act, not wait.I’ve seen struggling operations transform not because we had moremoney or talent, but because we started using our time differently. Westopped debating endlessly. We stopped tolerating inefficiency. We gotmoving—quickly, intelligently, together.Practical WisdomBlock time for what matters most—before the noise of the daytakes over.Evaluate your week: What drained your energy? What moved theneedle?Don’t confuse activity with productivity. Movement isn’t alwaysprogress.Learn to say no. Protect your time like you would your health oryour money.Make decisions. Perfectionism is a time thief disguised as highstandards.This principle—"use your time effectively"—is not just advice; it’s amindset. It's a refusal to drift. It's a commitment to live and lead withurgency, clarity, and purpose. Because in the end, we don’t managetime. We manage ourselves, within the time we have.

61Leadership Reflection: Use Your Time Effectively – We Don’t Have Time to WasteTime is the most democratic resource on earth—everyone gets 24 hoursa day. But how we use those hours defines our results, our legacy, andour lives.Early in my career, I learned a hard truth: wasted time is never neutral—it compounds negatively. It can cost you momentum, credibility,relationships, and opportunities that don’t come around twice.In leadership, time is not just a personal resource; it becomes amultiplier—or a bottleneck—for everyone who depends on you. Whenyou use time effectively, your team moves faster, decisions are sharper,and results accelerate. When you don’t, the ripple effect is damaging.Here’s what I’ve learned:Being busy is not the same as being productive. Filling yourcalendar is easy; delivering real value takes focus.Say no to the trivial many so you can say yes to the vital few.Decision delays are time thieves. It’s better to act with 80% claritythan to stagnate waiting for 100%.Protect your time ruthlessly. It is your most precious, non-renewable asset.One of the first signs of a high-performing team or leader is timediscipline. Meetings have a purpose. Priorities are clear. Energy goeswhere it matters most. I’ve seen organizations transform simply byshifting how they value and manage time.If there’s one takeaway from this chapter, let it be this: Don’t drift. Don’tdelay. Don’t waste time. Use it with intention—because we don’t get tochoose how much time we have, but we always get to choose how weuse it.

Key focus areas and lifelearnings Work hard whilst working smart having strong principles i.e. yourword must be your word. People can always trust you. Trust and Inspire people to achieve the best. Through that, youwill achieve more. Innovation will thrive. Be kind to people all the time. Be generous to people, because giving is better than receiving, butby giving you will reap abundantly. Don’t be shy to show that you are a Christian and that all thatyou have achieved came from Jesus. Never fly solo, the Holy Spirit was sent to guide us, lead us andprotect us. God's plans are always better than your plans. Have clear goals on what you want to achieve and applyconsistent behavior to achieve them. Have discipline in your life. Focus on doing the best every day. Enjoy life to the fullest but in a responsible manner. Be a Leader to people, not a manager. 64

 Difficult times will come your way like with all of us, butbelieve that you can overcome anything with God’s help. Remember that development in life comes from regularfailures, but we learn from it and go forward stronger. (Readthe book “Failing Forward”) Have discipline in life to lead a healthy lifestyle relating toFood and Exercise. Don’t let peer pressure influence you like with so many fallinginto that trap. Stick with your agreed principles and don’t be swayed bynegative behavior from your peers. Remind yourself regularly of where you find yourself in life.Are you living the life that you want to live? Your cost to a company should always be self-funding, hence mysavings to the company exceed my cost. Always recognize people’s good work and celebrate success. Don’t procrastinate on doing things immediately.Don’t worry about things you can’t control. Don’t compare yourself to other people. Don’t waste your time. Manage your time effectively. We don’t have the time to waste.65

Watch what you say. Your words carry incredible power. Discipline now, Freedom later. Discipline is critical. You must make your own happiness. Be content with yourself.While God is opening the next door, praise him in the hallway.Good communication is critical in all areas of your life. Control how you respond to people. Your habits and routines shape you. Never stop learning. Learn every day. Your integrity is non – negotiable. 66

67Bible verses relatable toyour career and Jobsituation “Commit your Plans to the Lord and your plans will succeed”– Proverbs 16:3  “I can do all things through him who gave strength”– Philippians 4:13 “It is well with the man who deals generously and lends whoconducts his affairs with justice” – Psalm 112:5 “Trust the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your ownunderstanding” 

68Many businesses have already implemented the use of AI technologieswith great success. Many companies lag and will find competing with AI-competent companies difficult. The pace of AI development is so rapidthat you will struggle to catch up to those who have implemented thistechnology. It was identified via a recent study that Company executiveboards are not up to speed with AI technologies, but they are entrustedto manage the Company. This is a big risk going forward for any Company, hence the requiredskills must be obtained rapidly.AI importance 

69Leading in the Age of AIWhen I started my journey as an apprentice, the tools of managementwere simple: a pen, a calculator, a clipboard, and a sharp mind.Decisions were driven by personal experience, intuition, and long hoursof discussion in boardrooms. Today, we are entering an era whereartificial intelligence (AI) can analyze mountains of data in seconds,forecast demand, optimize supply chains, and even draft businessstrategies.And yet, despite all this advancement, the essence of leadership has notchanged, it still requires vision, integrity, empathy, and the ability tomake sound decisions under uncertainty. What has changed is how welead.AI is not here to replace managers, it’s here to augment them. Theleaders who will thrive in this new era are those who can harness AI toenhance their decision-making, not outsource it. From customeranalytics and operational efficiency to talent management andpredictive forecasting, AI offers insights that were unimaginable just adecade ago.During my time leading large-scale operations and managing complexturnarounds, I often relied on data to guide strategic shifts. But astechnology advanced, I began to see how AI could do more than reporton the past, it could predict the future. Machine learning models couldhighlight inefficiencies we couldn’t see. Chatbots could handlethousands of customer queries in real time. Algorithms could identifywhich markets were ripest for expansion.However, the challenge was never the technology, it was the mindset.Many seasoned managers resisted change, not because they didn’tunderstand the technology, but because they feared it would make theirroles obsolete. That fear is misplaced. AI cannot replace leadership. Itcannot inspire a team during tough times. It cannot resolve conflictsrooted in emotion. It cannot replace the human touch in negotiations orthe gut feeling that tells you something is off despite what the numberssay.

70Preparing for the FutureAs a leader, your responsibility is to be curious, not necessarilytechnical. You don’t need to be a data scientist, but you do need to askthe right questions:What data are we not using today that could improve our decision-making?How can automation free up my team to focus on higher-valuework?Where can predictive analytics help us anticipate change instead ofreacting to it?The most powerful trait in the age of AI is not technical mastery, it’sadaptability. The ability to lead people through uncertainty. Thehumility to learn from tools that may know more than you. And thewisdom to know when not to rely solely on a machine’srecommendation.

71Leadership Lessons for the AI AgeLead with humanity: AI is powerful, but people still follow leaders,not algorithms. Empathy remains non-negotiable.Be data-informed, not data-blind: Let AI guide you, but don’t let itreplace your judgment.Champion lifelong learning: Encourage yourself and your teams tounderstand the basics of AI and data analytics. You don’t need toknow how it works, just what it can do.Use AI to elevate people, not replace them: Great leaders usetechnology to unlock their teams’ potential, not to diminish it.“AI will not replace managers. But managers who use AI will replacethose who don’t.” ~ Adapted from Andrew NgAs I look back on my journey from apprentice to Managing Director, Isee leadership not as a destination, but as a constant evolution. Theleaders of tomorrow will be those who embrace change, not just inmarkets and products, but in mindset and method. AI is not a threat,it’s a tool. The question is: will you be the one wielding it, or the onebeing replaced by those who do?

The Digital Edge in LeadershipWhen I sit back now as a managing director, it’s almost surreal to recallhow bewildered I was by the idea of artificial intelligence early in mycareer. Back then, spreadsheets and email were cutting-edge; anythingresembling “AI” felt like science fiction. Yet, the tide of technology hasswept through the boardroom. Today’s leaders are inundated with dataand decisions, in fact, research shows that 85% of executives report“decision stress,” with the volume of decisions exploding in recent years. I’ve learned that AI isn’t magic, but a powerful tool to help manage thatpressure. Looking back, I see how reluctant I once was and how embracing AIbecame pivotal in my own leadership journey.72The future is going todemand more techliteracy than ever before.

73From Doubt to DiscoveryIn those early years, I trusted my gut more than any computer. I pridedmyself on intuition and personal judgment. When a colleague firstmentioned machine learning or a “data-driven strategy,” I shrugged itoff. It reminded me of an old anecdote: in the late 1990s, evencompetent business owners dismissed the Internet as a fad, vowing “I’mnever getting on the Internet”. We all know what happened – those who held out lost out. In my case,I nearly repeated that mistake with AI. I’ll admit it: I was skeptical anduneasy about “black box” algorithms making decisions. But slowly, real-world pressures forced me to question that skepticism. My turning pointcame when our company’s data started choking on itself. Sales figures,customer info, and forecasts were scattered across disconnectedsystems. I remember grappling with fragmented, unreliable data andslow, error-prone reports – just like the manager at Sparex whodescribed “fragmented data” and “inefficient reporting”. Inevitably, we hit a bottleneck in a budget review: I could see onlysmoke through the fog. That crisis drove me to explore analytics tools.To my surprise, what I found was liberating. We implemented a businessintelligence platform that consolidated our data, giving us a singlesource of truth. Overnight, our dull spreadsheets transformed intodynamic dashboards. I saw colleagues light up as they ran their ownanalyses. It echoed what one industry expert said: data, whenharnessed effectively, is “the lifeblood of modern organizations,”empowering better decisions and innovation. It was a lesson in humility, my intuition was valuable, but it neededthe guidance of clean, analyzed data.

74Embracing the AlgorithmWith momentum on our side, I began applying more AI-driven tools toroutine problems. For example, forecasting became a collaborationbetween human insight and machine learning. We rolled out apredictive analytics model for inventory management. Before, I wouldrely on past experience and gut feelings to estimate demand. Now, Icould plug in up-to-date variables and watch the model churn outprojections with confidence intervals. The result? Fewer stockouts,smoother production planning, and a clear sense of what was drivingdemand. Colleagues who once resisted began to trust the numbers. We essentially consolidated our disparate databases into one platform,much like Sparex did, and that “enabled real-time insights and predictiveanalytics” to accelerate decision-making. I still remember thinking, Sothis is what faster, smarter decisions feel like. Even mundane tasks goteasier. I started using AI to automate repetitive work: schedulingmeetings, drafting summary emails, or generating basic reports. Whatused to take hours of manual effort became almost instantaneous. This freed our team to focus on strategy and people, rather than dataentry. A benchmark research I saw years later crystallized why I felt somotivated to adapt: data-driven organizations are three times morelikely to report significant improvements in decision-making than others.In hindsight, turning to data wasn’t just a fad, it was a necessity. It wassobering to realize I had been trying to play on an uneven field, relyingonly on instinct while competitors used data like a secret weapon.

75Lessons Learned and AdviceLooking back on that phase of my career, a few lessons stand out. First, Ilearned never to dismiss new tools out of hand. If anything, I became alittle paranoid about being a “have-not.” One analyst’s words still ring inmy ear: “The new haves and have-nots are going to be businesses thatembraced AI and those that didn’t… if you’re not on the train, it’sdistancing itself from you every single day”. That turned out to be true in our industry. By the time I becamedirector, surveys found that nearly 78% of companies already used AI insome form. We had joined the 22% or so left behind. I didn’t want mycompany to be the one caught off-guard as competitors sprinted ahead.Another big lesson was balance: AI is a tool, not a replacement forleadership. There’s a quote I came across about data and intuitionsaying the best decisions combine both. I live by that now. I learned touse AI outputs as powerful input, but then apply judgment, context, andvalues. For instance, an algorithm might flag customer churn risk, but Iknow my best team members and their customers, and that humantouch matters too. So I’d take the AI’s analysis and then use my peopleskills to turn it into action. This resonates with what data experts advise: use data for speed andaccuracy, but maintain the human element in decision-making. In fact,one perspective even notes that leaders buried under data shouldsometimes trust a seasoned gut feeling. I found that truth again in myjourney – AI amplified my effectiveness, but it never replaced thenecessity of understanding my team and clients. To any aspiringmanager reading this, my advice is clear: become comfortable with dataand AI early. Don’t wait until you’re pushed. Start small if you must –use a simple analytics tool or experiment with an AI-powered assistant. Iwas heartened to learn that over half of top executives already usegenerative AI regularly. In other words, the people running businessestoday expect their leaders to know this stuff. Cultivate a data-drivenmindset: question decisions with “What does the data say?” andencourage your team to back arguments with evidence. At the sametime, don’t let fear shut you down. 

76Change can be scary, but the true risk is stagnation. Remember thateven storied managers of the past had to adopt new methods; the wiseones did, and the others faded away. Finally, use AI to empower yourpeople rather than to control them. In my own transition, I consciouslyput my team at the center, teaching them how to use the new tools andencouraging feedback. I learned that adopting AI is as much a culturalchange as a technical one. You need to build trust around it. Whenquestions of “I don’t understand this model” or “Is the AI making alldecisions?” arose, I made sure to explain and demystify. Over time, Isaw creativity flourish once the routine burden was lifted. My team began brainstorming even bigger improvements, no longerbogged down by manual work. This collaborative approach paiddividends.Artificial intelligence isnot a substitute forhuman intelligence, it isa tool to amplify humancreativity and ingenuity. 

77Looking AheadNow, as I reflect on rising from apprentice to managing director, I seethe role of AI as part of a broader leadership arc. Embracing AI tools waslike learning a new language – initially awkward, but ultimately givingmy leadership voice more reach. It sharpened my vision and freed me tofocus on the human side of management: inspiring people, settinggoals, and crafting strategy. In the grand scheme, technology changes, and tools will evolve again,but the core of leadership remains constant. The best leaders remaincurious, open to learning, and ready to integrate whatever helps theirteam excel. In the end, the journey taught me that data and AI are notjust technical topics but reflections of good leadership values: courageto change, humility to learn, and wisdom to blend insight with intuition.Just as I once mentored junior colleagues in their early careers, I nowmentor others in embracing AI. I tell them that AI is a powerful compass,but a compass needs a steady hand at the helm. As long as managers remember to couple high-tech tools with high-touch leadership, they’ll not only survive the digital revolution – they’lllead it.

Conclusion / Summary “It was a great honor for me to write this book about my career.” I really hope that the message of this book came clearly to young people out there and that they will realize that all things are possible if you focus and put your mind to it, but with lots of hard work. It is clear in this book that humble and poor beginnings should not deter what you can achieve in life. God made you unique (only one like you) to achieve the goals that you have set. It is also clear that networking and learning during your career will benefit you later. Understand that the “No pain no Gain “slogan applies but in the end, you will reap the benefits. It must be clearly understood that optimizing your studies is of significant importance. School graduation opens a door, and adiploma opens another few, but your degree or additionalachievements will open many doors for you to choose from. 78

Remember study whilst you are young, it is a once-off period, but itcomes with benefits for a lifetime, and it promotes you to a higherearnings level. The statistics show it clearly. By achieving your goals other doors i.e. Overseas trips will be possible which takes you into a new world of learning. The stability of your family life is an integral part of your development and success. If it was not for my wife and lovely children I would not have achieved, what I did. Today I not only have my Wife and children, but 9 Grand Children and soon 3 Great Grand Children. What a blessing and honor!I worked for many companies in my career especially later due to short contract work. However, the experience that I have gained via all these companies boosted my general knowledge tremendously which I could apply in any Company. I cannot over emphasize the importance of adopting the Management style of “Trust and Inspire”. The old style of Command and Control is no longer appropriate in Business today. People must be free to develop and add value to businesses. Don’t fall into the trap of “poor management” as described in this book. Don’t be afraid to make mistakes as that is how you failforward  and make progress. 79

I was truly fortunate to have the unique moments in my life asdescribed. Take note of the section on Business and Life’s learnings and apply these suggestions. Finally, I wrote this book while being supported by my wife. Thisbook is dedicated to my children and grandchildren. I hope thatthey will apply this in their future careers. 80Last, but definitely not least the role that my parents and siblingsplayed in my journey.
